solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int findUnsortedSubarray(int[] nums) {
        int n = nums.length;
        int start = 0, end = n - 1;

        // Find the first element from the left that is out of order
        while (start < n - 1 && nums[start] <= nums[start + 1]) {
            start++;
        }

        // If the array is already sorted
        if (start == n - 1) {
            return 0;
        }

        // Find the first element from the right that is out of order
        while (end > 0 && nums[end] >= nums[end - 1]) {
            end--;
        }

        // Find the min and max in the unsorted subarray
        int subMin = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        int subMax = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
        for (int i = start; i <= end; i++) {
            subMin = Math.min(subMin, nums[i]);
            subMax = Math.max(subMax, nums[i]);
        }

        // Extend the start to the left to include any elements greater than the subMin
        while (start > 0 && nums[start - 1] > subMin) {
            start--;
        }

        // Extend the end to the right to include any elements less than the subMax
        while (end < n - 1 && nums[end + 1] < subMax) {
            end++;
        }

        return end - start + 1;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The algorithm identifies the smallest unsorted subarray by finding the first and last misplaced elements and then extending the subarray to include any elements outside that range which violate the sorted order. It operates in linear time and uses constant space, making it highly efficient.
